title: Unifying spacetime approaches to quantum mechanics
authors: N. L. Diaz, M. Cerezo, Paolo Braccia
abstract: Recent efforts to formulate quantum mechanics in a way that treats space and time on a more equal footing have led to a large variety of spacetime-oriented approaches. In this work we present a detailed study of spacetime states, the objects that play the role of quantum states in the recently introduced framework of spacetime quantum mechanics, and show that the main proposals in the literature are different manifestations of the same underlying object. Path integrals, quantum states over time, pseudo-density matrices, the Page and Wootters mechanism, superdensity operators, and timelike-entanglement proposals all arise from spacetime states through particular evaluations, reduced information, linear maps, or quantum channels. This unification provides explicit mathematical representations of these formalisms, reveals relations among them, and clarifies the spacetime information each one captures. We also study the broader relevance of the spacetime-state point of view for Leggett-Garg inequalities, OTOCs, temporal tensor networks, fermionic systems, relativistic QFTs, quantum reference frames, and classical physics, together with additional insights and perspectives revealed by the common unifying framework.
summary: The paper proposes 'spacetime quantum mechanics' (SQM) to resolve the conflict between space and time in QM by defining generalized 'spacetime states.' It demonstrates that various existing formalisms—like path integrals and pseudo-density matrices—are mathematically equivalent manifestations of this single underlying state object. This unification provides deep insights into how temporal correlations govern quantum dynamics.
main_problem: The central problem is formulating quantum mechanics (QM) in a way that treats space and time symmetrically, addressing the conceptual tension between relativity and canonical QM.
main_result: The authors unify numerous existing spacetime approaches by showing they all derive from a single underlying mathematical object: the 'spacetime state' ($R$).
method: They develop a formalism using a finite-time tensor product Hilbert space ($\mathcal{H} =  igotimes_{t=0}^{N-1} \mathcal{H}_t$) and define spacetime states as generalized quantum operators that encode temporal correlations.
model_or_system: The model is 'spacetime quantum mechanics' (SQM), describing systems over a finite spacetime region. The formalism uses time translation operators ($\mathbf{e}^{i\epsilon P}$) and constructs the state $R$ from initial states $ho_0$.
key_observables: Spacetime states ($R$), pseudo-entropies ($S_\alpha(R)$), Wightman functions, and Out-of-Time-Order Correlators (OTOCs).
important_parameters: Finite time window $T = \epsilon N$, the time step $\epsilon$, and system/environment bipartitions.
main_assumptions: The initial formulation assumes a finite time window and discrete time slices, although extensions to continuum limits are discussed. The derivation of certain properties relies on specific mathematical decompositions (e.g., for pure states).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the relationship between standard QM and spacetime approaches; one shows spectral analysis of $R$ across bipartitions, while another plots OTOC eigenvalues versus time.
paper_structure: The paper builds by first defining the necessary mathematical machinery (Spacetime Hilbert space, action operators $\mathbf{S}$), then deriving how standard quantum evolution pictures (Schrödinger/Heisenberg) are recovered via partial tracing, and finally analyzing advanced properties like isospectrality and entanglement measures.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, unified mathematical framework for understanding time evolution in quantum theory, which is crucial for developing consistent theories of quantum gravity or relativistic QFTs.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic depiction of how standard QM and its main extensions to a spacetime setting arise from a common object.

FIG. 2. Tensor network representation of the main objects of the formalism for N = 3. Panel a) Time translations across slices and the corresponding SWAP decomposition. Panel b) Time translations within slices. Panel c) the quantum action operator and a particular case of Theorem 1.

FIG. 3. Spectrum in the complex plane of RS = TrE[R] for S, E qubits and N = 2. We sampled Haar randomly both global unitaries and (i) Separable system-environment and pure state (green points) (ii) Separable system-environment and mixed state (orange points) (iii) General (entangled) system-environment state (blue points). For each scenario a total of 104 points have been sampled. In case (i) the spec- trum is restricted to the real interval (0, 1) while in case (ii) to (−1/2, 1). In the general case (iii) the spectrum is complex.

FIG. 4. Contour plot of the pseudo-purity Tr[R2 S] with initial state given by |ψ(θ)⟩= cos(θ/4)|00⟩+sin(θ/4)|11⟩and global unitary U = (1S ⊗|0⟩E⟨0| + XS ⊗|1⟩E⟨1|)e−iαYE/4. Here, θ parameterizes the initial state entanglement while α parame- terizes the entanglement generated by the evolution (and the final state entanglement). In particular, the line θ = 0 cor- responds to the standard purity of the state of the system after the evolution. In this scenario the pseudo-purity is a real quantity for all values of the parameters since the com- plex eigenvalues of RS come in conjugate pairs.

FIG. 5. Contour plot of the imagitivity of the spacetime state RA corresponding to the ground state of a critical antiferromagnetic Heisenberg model under bipartition A = (0, 0) ∪(t, x) B = rest. Here we are considering the same number of temporal and spatial slices N = L + 1 = 101 with ϵ = 0.05. The red dash-doted lines correspond to the speed of low-energy modes associated with an effective infrared CFT description.
